---
name: approved-spec-to-pr
description: >-
  Takes a human-approved spec file, implements it, delegates test generation,
  test review, and code review to separate AI agents, loops on corrections, and
  opens a pull request for human merge.
user-invocable: true
---

# Approved spec to pull request

You are the **orchestrator**. You coordinate multiple AI agents and own the
workflow state, but you do not merge the PR.

The workflow is:

1. Validate the approved spec.
2. Implement the spec.
3. Use a separate AI agent to generate tests.
4. Use a third AI agent to review tests for gaps and weak assertions.
5. Use a fourth AI agent to review the full diff.
6. Create a pull request only after the reviews pass.
7. Loop from any failing review back to the previous responsible step.

## Input

The user must provide the path to one approved spec file generated by the
`issue-to-spec` skill, for example:

```text
docs\specs\123-add-empty-state.md
```

If no spec path is provided, ask for exactly one spec path before continuing.

## Hard gates

Stop before implementation if any of these are true:

- The spec file does not exist.
- The spec status is not clearly `Approved`.
- The spec contains unresolved open questions that block implementation.
- The working tree contains unrelated changes that would be overwritten.
- The repository cannot authenticate to GitHub with `gh auth status`.

Never create a PR with known blocking test-review or code-review findings.
Never merge the PR. Human merge is the final gate.

## Preflight

Run from the repository root with PowerShell paths.

1. Confirm the spec exists and read it.
2. Confirm the spec contains an approved status, such as:
   ```markdown
   **Status:** Approved
   ```
3. Check GitHub auth:
   ```powershell
   gh auth status
   ```
4. Check current repo:
   ```powershell
   gh repo view --json nameWithOwner --jq .nameWithOwner
   ```
5. Check worktree:
   ```powershell
   git --no-pager status --short
   ```
6. Identify linked issues from the spec `References` section and body. Prefer
   explicit GitHub issue URLs or `#<number>` references.
7. Create a branch before changing code:
   ```powershell
   git switch -c spec\<issue-number-or-slug>
   ```

If there are existing unrelated changes, stop and ask the user how to proceed.
Do not overwrite or revert user work.

## Phase 1: Implementation agent

Delegate to a first AI agent with the `task` tool:

- `agent_type: "general-purpose"`
- Recommended model: `claude-sonnet-4.6`

Give the agent the full spec content, repository path, branch name, linked issue,
and these instructions:

```text
Implement only the approved spec. Do not expand product scope. Read the relevant
code before editing. Follow repository conventions. Preserve existing behavior
unless the spec explicitly changes it.

Make the application changes needed by the spec. Do not create the pull request.
Do not merge. Prefer not to write tests in this phase unless a tiny smoke check is
needed to keep the implementation coherent; a separate test-generation agent will
own the real tests.

After editing, run the smallest relevant validation commands available for the
touched surface. Report changed files, commands run, commands not run and why,
and any uncertainty.
```

When the implementation agent finishes, inspect `git status --short` and the
diff. If it changed files outside the spec scope, send it back once with a
specific scope-correction prompt. If it still remains out of scope, stop and
report the blocker.

## Phase 2: Test generation agent

Delegate to a second AI agent with the `task` tool:

- `agent_type: "general-purpose"`
- Recommended model: `claude-sonnet-4.6`

Give it the full spec, implementation diff, linked issue, and these instructions:

```text
Generate meaningful tests for the implemented behavior. Tests must fail if the
requested behavior is broken or reverted. Avoid vanity tests, snapshots that only
assert rendering exists, and assertions that do not prove the acceptance criteria.

Use the repository's existing test framework and test locations. For frontend
behavior, prefer focused Vitest/RTL tests. For Temporal behavior, add pytest
tests under temporal/tests. For Supabase/database behavior, add migration/seed
validation or contract tests only if the repository already has that style.

Do not expand product scope. Do not create a PR. Report test files changed and
validation commands run.
```

After this phase, run or ask the agent to run the relevant test command if it did
not already do so. Keep failures visible; do not hide them with broad catches or
silent fallbacks.

## Phase 3: Test-review agent

Delegate to a third AI agent with the `task` tool:

- `agent_type: "rubber-duck"` or `agent_type: "code-review"`
- Recommended model: `claude-sonnet-4.6`

Give it only the approved spec, the implementation summary, and the test diff.
Instruct it not to modify files:

```text
Review only the tests. Look for missing acceptance criteria coverage, weak
assertions, vanity/existence-only tests, tests that would pass if the feature
were broken, brittle over-mocking, missing edge cases, and tests in the wrong
layer.

Return one of:
- PASS: tests are meaningful and adequate.
- CHANGES_REQUESTED: list specific test gaps and the exact behavior/assertions
  that must be added or strengthened.

Do not comment on style unless it affects correctness. Do not modify files.
```

### Test-review loop

If the test-review agent returns `CHANGES_REQUESTED`, loop back to Phase 2.

- Give the test-generation agent the exact findings.
- Ask it to update only the tests unless the finding proves implementation
  behavior is missing.
- Run Phase 3 again.
- Limit this loop to 3 cycles. If the tests still do not pass review after 3
  cycles, stop and report the unresolved findings.

If the test review reveals that the implementation itself is incomplete, loop
back to Phase 1 with the specific implementation gap, then repeat Phases 2 and 3.

## Phase 4: Validation

Run the relevant available checks before final code review. Choose by touched
surface:

```powershell
npm --prefix frontend run lint
npm --prefix frontend run build
npm --prefix frontend test -- --run
python -m pytest temporal/tests
supabase db reset --config supabase\config.toml
```

Run only commands that are relevant and available. If a command is missing,
requires unavailable services, or fails due to a known environment limitation,
record that explicitly for the PR body.

## Phase 5: Code-review agent

Delegate to a fourth AI agent with the `task` tool:

- `agent_type: "code-review"` for review-only feedback, or
- `agent_type: "general-purpose"` if you need the same final agent to both
  review and create the PR after passing review.
- Recommended model: `claude-sonnet-4.6`

Give it the approved spec, linked issue, full diff, test-review result, and
validation output. Instruct it:

```text
Review the full diff against the approved spec. Check correctness, scope control,
security, data safety, error handling, type safety, backward compatibility, and
test adequacy. Treat the approved spec as the source of truth.

Return one of:
- PASS: no blocking issues; PR can be created.
- CHANGES_REQUESTED: list concrete blocking issues, each with file/function
  evidence and the required fix.

Do not request style-only changes. Do not merge.
```

### Code-review loop

If the code-review agent returns `CHANGES_REQUESTED`:

- For implementation defects, loop back to Phase 1.
- For test-only defects, loop back to Phase 2.
- After fixes, repeat test generation/review as needed, rerun validation, and
  rerun Phase 5.
- Limit this loop to 3 cycles. If blocking findings remain after 3 cycles, stop
  and report the findings instead of creating a PR.

## Phase 6: Commit and create pull request

Create the PR only when:

- Spec status is `Approved`.
- Implementation is in scope.
- Test review is `PASS`.
- Code review is `PASS`.
- Relevant validation has been run or limitations are documented.

Commit changes with a short imperative message and the required trailer:

```text
<imperative summary>

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>
```

Push the branch and create a PR:

```powershell
git push -u origin HEAD
gh pr create --title "<short imperative title>" --body-file <pr-body-file>
```

The PR body must include:

- What changed
- Why it changed
- Approved spec path
- Linked issue, if any
- Tests/checks run
- Checks not run and why
- Test-review result
- Code-review result
- Risk and rollback notes
- Docs updated, or why no docs were needed
- Human merge note: `Human review and merge required.`

Do not enable auto-merge.

## Final response

Report:

- spec path used,
- branch name,
- PR URL,
- validation commands run,
- whether any checks were skipped,
- confirmation that the PR is waiting for human review/merge.

If stopped before PR creation, report the exact phase and blocker.
